options.C: Harriet has no viable challenge because the WUDC acted within its statutory powers by completing a compulsory purchase and the courts cannot review the merits of that decision.
options.D: Harriet has grounds for challenge based on fettering of discretion alone; the purpose behind the acquisition is irrelevant to the lawfulness of the compulsory purchase since the WUDC has an express statutory power to acquire land.
options.E: Harriet has no grounds for challenge on improper purpose because the WUDC will generate income which can be reinvested into future regeneration projects, meaning the ultimate purpose is consistent with the statutory objective.

questions.explanation: A is correct. On fettering of discretion: a statutory body may use a policy to guide its exercise of discretion, but it must always be willing to consider the individual merits of a case and to depart from the policy in exceptional circumstances. A policy applied 'without exception and regardless of any individual merits' fetters the discretion Parliament conferred. On improper purpose: a body can only exercise its statutory powers for the purpose for which Parliament granted them. The Urban Renewal Act grants powers for urban regeneration; using those powers to acquire land for a private commercial park generating income for the WUDC is a use of power for an improper — and indeed self-serving — purpose. The fact that income might incidentally fund future regeneration does not convert an improper primary purpose into a lawful one. B: Incorrect — the courts can and do review the lawfulness of the exercise of statutory powers; acting within the four corners of the Act in form does not excuse using powers for an improper purpose. A: Incorrect — improper purpose is an independent ground of review that can vitiate a decision regardless of whether the body has the formal power; Harriet's strongest argument includes both grounds. C: Incorrect — bad faith is not required to establish fettering of discretion or improper purpose; both are objective grounds of illegality. E: Incorrect — the dominant or primary purpose must be the statutory purpose; a speculative and incidental connection to future regeneration does not legitimise the exercise of a power whose immediate and real purpose is income generation.

questions.tag: Duty to act fairly and legitimate expectation — procedural
questions.scenario: The Alnwick Pharmaceutical Licensing Board (APLB) has for ten years sent all licence holders a written notice of any proposed changes to licence conditions, followed by a thirty-day consultation window during which representations could be made. The APLB now proposes to vary the conditions of Cressida's licence — which she has held for twelve years — by adding a requirement that she must submit quarterly financial returns under the new Pharmaceutical Accountability (Amendment) Regulations 2025. The APLB sent no notice to Cressida and implemented the variation immediately, citing an 'administrative emergency' arising from a regulatory scandal elsewhere in the sector. Cressida argues that she should have been consulted before the variation was made.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Cressida on whether and how she can challenge the variation of her licence conditions?

options.A: Cressida has strong grounds on two bases: a procedural legitimate expectation from the APLB's consistent ten-year practice of providing notice and a hearing before licence revocation; and a failure to follow that procedure constitutes unfairness.
options.B: Cressida has no basis for challenge because the APLB acted under the authority of the 2025 Regulations and procedural fairness cannot override a statutory obligation to vary licence conditions.
options.C: Cressida has grounds for challenge only if she can demonstrate that the variation of her licence conditions causes her financial loss greater than the regulatory benefit achieved.
options.D: Cressida has grounds for challenge based on legitimate expectation alone; the duty to act fairly does not apply because the APLB was implementing a regulatory requirement, not making a discretionary decision.
options.E: Cressida has no basis for challenge because the administrative emergency justification is a matter of executive judgment that courts cannot review.

questions.explanation: A is correct. On procedural legitimate expectation: the APLB's established ten-year practice of notice and consultation before any variation constitutes a regular past practice capable of generating a legitimate expectation that the procedure would continue (GCHQ; IRC v Unilever). The body cannot simply abandon an established practice without compelling reasons; vague reference to a regulatory scandal elsewhere — with no direct connection to Cressida — is unlikely to be a sufficiently compelling reason to justify abandoning the established procedure. On the duty of procedural fairness: separately from legitimate expectation, the common law requires that a person be given notice and an opportunity to make representations before a decision that adversely affects their existing legal rights is taken. Varying a twelve-year licence holder's conditions without any prior notice is a paradigm breach of this duty. B: Incorrect — the 2025 Regulations provide the substantive power to impose financial returns, but they do not suspend the common law duty to act fairly in exercising that power; unless the Regulations expressly exclude procedural fairness, it applies. C: Incorrect — the duty to act fairly is not confined to discretionary decisions; it applies wherever an administrative decision adversely affects a person's existing legal rights or legitimate expectations. D: Incorrect — the 'administrative emergency' rationale is not immune from review; the court can examine whether it was genuine, proportionate and sufficient to override established procedural protections. E: Incorrect — the right to procedural fairness is not contingent on demonstrating a financial threshold of loss; the right is to a fair process, not to compensation for the outcome.

questions.scenario: In 2019, the Solway Housing Authority (SHA) entered into a written agreement with fifteen families living in a purpose-built residential estate, which stated: 'The SHA gives an unambiguous commitment that the residents of Solway Crescent will be entitled to remain in their homes for as long as they wish to do so.' The families, relying on this commitment, invested significant personal funds in adapting their properties and declining to pursue other housing options. In 2025, facing severe budget pressures, the SHA announces that it intends to close Solway Crescent and rehouse all fifteen families in temporary accommodation across the city. Preethi, one of the residents, seeks judicial review.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Preethi on whether and how she can challenge the SHA's decision?

options.A: Preethi has a basis for challenge only if she can show that the SHA acted irrationally in making the 2019 commitment, since the expectation only becomes enforceable if it was reasonable for the SHA to have made the promise in the first place.
options.B: Preethi has no basis for challenge because public bodies must be free to change their policies in response to budget pressures, and any expectation of a permanent right to remain in public housing is inherently unreasonable.
options.C: Preethi has a basis for challenge, but the court can only require the SHA to carry out a fresh consultation with the residents before proceeding; it cannot enforce the substantive right to remain because this would fetter the SHA's ongoing discretion in housing allocation.
options.D: Preethi has a strong basis for challenge — the SHA's written commitment was a clear, specific, and unambiguous promise to an identifiable group on which they detrimentally relied; departing from it without compelling justification is unlawful.
options.E: Preethi has a strong basis for challenge, but she would need to show that the SHA's decision was Wednesbury unreasonable; the court will only intervene if no reasonable housing authority could have decided to close the estate.

questions.explanation: D is correct. This scenario closely mirrors ex parte Coughlan, where an unambiguous promise made to a small group of identifiable individuals who detrimentally relied on it was held to give rise to a substantive legitimate expectation that could be enforced by the court. The key features are present: the promise was clear, specific and unconditional; it was made to an identifiable group (fifteen families); and there was detrimental reliance (financial investment and foregone alternatives). Under the third Coughlan category, the court can assess whether frustrating the expectation amounts to an abuse of power, weighing the degree of unfairness to the residents against the public interest justification offered. Budget pressures alone are unlikely to justify the severe breach of an unconditional commitment. C: Incorrect — Coughlan establishes that substantive legitimate expectations can be enforced where the promise is sufficiently clear and individual reliance is detrimental; 'policy change needed' is not an automatic trump. D: Incorrect — the enforceability of the expectation does not depend on showing the original promise was irrational; the question is whether resiling from it now is an abuse of power. B: Incorrect — while the court might in some cases order a fresh consultation, Coughlan confirms that courts can go further and enforce the substantive expectation itself where the promise was unconditional and the reliance severe. E: Incorrect — under the Coughlan framework, Wednesbury unreasonableness is the lowest level of review (Category A); a clear substantive expectation arising from an unconditional individual promise attracts Category C review — whether frustration was so unfair as to be an abuse of power — not simple Wednesbury.

questions.tag: Section 3 and section 4 — compatibility and declarations
questions.scenario: Section 7(2) of the Residential Tenancy Control Act provides that: 'A landlord shall not be required to provide reasons for a decision to terminate a tenancy where the tenancy is held by a person who is not a citizen of this jurisdiction.' Ibrahim is a long-term legal resident who has lived in the same rented property for eleven years. His landlord — a local authority — has terminated his tenancy without providing any reasons. Ibrahim argues that s 7(2), as applied to him, is incompatible with his rights under Article 8 ECHR (the right to respect for private and family life and home) and Article 14 ECHR (prohibition of discrimination in the enjoyment of Convention rights).
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Ibrahim on the legal options available to him and the likely remedies?

options.A: Ibrahim has strong grounds — under s 3 HRA the court must interpret s 7(2) compatibly with Arts 8 and 14 so far as possible; if s 3 cannot achieve compatibility, a s 4 declaration of incompatibility may be made.
options.B: Ibrahim's only remedy is a declaration under s 4 HRA, since the local authority was acting in accordance with primary legislation; s 3 cannot be used to alter the clear words of a statute.
options.C: Ibrahim should apply directly to the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg, since UK domestic courts cannot provide effective remedies for incompatible primary legislation.
options.D: Ibrahim has no remedy because the local authority was complying with s 7(2) of the Residential Tenancy Control Act and a public authority that acts in accordance with primary legislation cannot breach s 6(1) HRA.
options.E: Ibrahim has a strong claim under s 6(1) HRA because local authorities are public authorities; the s 6(2) statutory defence is irrelevant where the Convention rights engaged are as fundamental as Arts 8 and 14.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The analysis requires careful sequencing of the HRA machinery. The starting point is s 3: the court has a strong duty to read s 7(2) compatibly with Arts 8 and 14, including reading down the citizenship restriction or implying in a right to reasons for long-term residents, so far as it is possible without going against the grain of the legislation (Ghaidan). If s 3 achieves compatibility, the s 6(2) defence falls away and Ibrahim has a remedy under s 8 HRA against the local authority. If s 3 cannot achieve compatibility, the court makes a s 4 declaration, but this does not affect the validity of s 7(2) or give Ibrahim a direct remedy — the s 6(2) defence protects the authority that acted in accordance with inalterable primary legislation. A: Incorrect — A is right that s 6(2) can protect a public authority acting under primary legislation, but it ignores the s 3 duty, which is the first step and may eliminate the s 6(2) defence. C: Incorrect — domestic remedies must first be exhausted before the ECtHR can be petitioned; moreover, domestic courts can and must apply s 3 and s 4 first. D: Incorrect — this treats s 4 as the first resort when it should be the last; s 3 must be attempted first, and the courts have shown a willingness to adopt linguistically strained readings to achieve compatibility. E: Incorrect — the s 6(2) defence is explicitly available regardless of the fundamental nature of the rights engaged; if primary legislation requires the action, the defence applies unless s 3 can achieve a compatible reading.

questions.tag: Article 5 — deprivation of liberty and safeguards
questions.scenario: The Public Safety (Emergency Powers) Act (PSEP Act) grants the Commissioner of the National Safety Directorate (NSD) the power to issue 'restriction notices' preventing named individuals from attending public gatherings 'where there are reasonable grounds to believe the individual may contribute to public disorder'. Restriction notices last for up to 72 hours. Leo, a political activist with no criminal record, was served with a restriction notice before a lawfully organised public protest, on the basis of an intelligence report that he 'had attended previous protests that became disorderly'. He was detained by NSD officers for 30 hours when he attempted to attend the protest in breach of the notice. Leo argues that both the restriction notice regime and his detention violate Art 5 ECHR.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Leo on whether and how he can challenge the restriction notice regime and his detention?

options.A: Leo has no basis for challenge because Art 5(1)(c) expressly permits detention to prevent the commission of an offence, and the NSD's assessment of reasonable grounds is a matter of executive judgment that courts cannot review.
options.B: Leo has no basis for challenge because Art 5 does not apply to pre-emptive measures designed to prevent public disorder, and the PSEP Act provides a lawful basis for the restriction notice.
options.C: Leo's only viable claim is under Art 5(4) for failure to provide a speedy review of his detention; he has no claim under Art 5(1) because the PSEP Act provides a statutory basis, which is all that is required for a lawful deprivation of liberty.
options.D: Leo has a strong challenge under Art 5, but only in relation to the duration of detention; the restriction notice itself does not engage Art 5 because it does not amount to a deprivation of liberty.
options.E: Leo has grounds under Art 5 on multiple bases: the detention constitutes a deprivation of liberty; Art 5(1)(c) permits preventive detention only where reasonably necessary; and Arts 5(2)-(4) require prompt notification, judicial supervision, and speedy review.

questions.explanation: E is correct. This question requires analysis across multiple Art 5 safeguards. On Art 5(1): a restriction notice preventing attendance at gatherings, combined with 30-hour detention on breach, is likely to constitute a deprivation of liberty, not merely a restriction on movement; the cumulative effect matters. For Art 5(1)(c) — detention to prevent an offence — Ostendorf and Hicks (affirmed in S, V & A v Denmark) permit short-term preventative detention, but the standard of 'reasonably considered necessary' must be objectively satisfied. Attending previous protests that became disorderly is an extremely weak evidential basis for individualised reasonable grounds; it risks being a form of guilt by association rather than specific reasonable suspicion about Leo himself (Fox, Campbell and Hartley). On Art 5(2): Leo must be informed promptly and in sufficient detail of the reasons for his detention. On Art 5(4): he is entitled to speedy judicial review of his ongoing detention; 30 hours without any judicial oversight raises serious questions. D: Incorrect — Art 5 applies to all deprivations of liberty, including those justified by prevention of disorder; a statutory basis is necessary but not sufficient — the statutory basis itself must be Convention-compatible and the individual grounds must satisfy objective standards. C: Incorrect — the Art 5(1) lawfulness question is not satisfied merely by pointing to a statutory basis; the individual grounds, the objective suspicion standard, and the proportionality of the detention must all be assessed. E: Incorrect — the courts can and do review whether the 'reasonable grounds' standard has been objectively satisfied (Fox, Campbell and Hartley); executive assessment is not immune. B: Incorrect — the question of whether the restriction notice itself constitutes a deprivation of liberty is a live and important issue that should be assessed; combined with forced detention for breach, it is strongly arguable that Art 5 is engaged from the moment of the restriction notice's enforcement.

questions.tag: Article 6 — access to justice and fair trial
questions.scenario: The Professional Standards Tribunal (PST) is a statutory body that determines whether lawyers should be disbarred. Under the Legal Services Regulation Act, PST hearings are conducted in private by a panel of three members, all of whom are appointed by and can be removed at will by the Lord Chancellor. There is no right of appeal from a PST decision. Valentina, a barrister, has been disbarred by the PST without being given any written reasons for the decision and without access to the evidence relied on against her. She seeks to challenge the proceedings.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Valentina on whether and how she can challenge the PST proceedings?

options.A: Valentina has grounds under Art 6: the disbarment determines civil rights engaging Art 6(1); the PST lacks independence; oral hearing and written reasons are required; and the absence of any right of appeal may compound the violation.
options.B: Valentina has no challenge because the PST proceedings are governed by statute and Parliament's design of a specialised regulatory process must be respected.
options.C: Valentina has no challenge under Art 6 because the right to a fair trial only applies in criminal proceedings, and disbarment is a civil regulatory matter.
options.D: Valentina has grounds for challenge on independence grounds only; the hearing procedures and absence of an appeal are matters for Parliament and are not reviewable under Art 6.
options.E: Valentina has a strong challenge under Art 6, but only in relation to the absence of written reasons; the other procedural features are within the PST's discretion and cannot be challenged.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Art 6(1) applies to the determination of both criminal charges and civil rights and obligations. The right to practise as a barrister is a civil right, so Art 6(1) is engaged by the disbarment proceedings. On independence: Findlay v UK established that the relevant test asks whether the body presents an appearance of independence, taking into account the manner of appointment and terms of office, existence of guarantees against outside pressures, and appearance of independence to an outside observer. The Lord Chancellor's power to appoint and remove members at will, with no fixed tenure, creates a structural dependence on the executive that fails this test. On access to a court: Art 6(1) requires effective access to a court capable of independently reviewing the determination; an absence of any appeal raises serious concerns. On fairness: not being given the evidence against her or any reasons for the decision prevents Valentina from understanding or challenging the case against her — a fundamental requirement of a fair hearing. B: Incorrect — parliamentary design of a statutory body does not exempt it from Art 6 compliance; Findlay v UK involved a statutory court martial system and still found a violation. D: Incorrect — the hearing procedures and lack of appeal are central components of Art 6's guarantee of a fair hearing and access to justice; they are directly reviewable. C: Incorrect — Art 6(1) expressly applies to determinations of 'civil rights and obligations', which includes the right to practise a regulated profession. E: Incorrect — while the absence of reasons is indeed a strong ground, the independence and access to court issues are equally strong and independent grounds; a single-issue restriction of the challenge would significantly understate the strength of Valentina's position.

questions.scenario: Under the Environmental Licensing (Industrial Processes) Act, the Environment Control Agency (ECA) has a power to revoke an operating licence where a licensee has 'persistently failed to comply with environmental standards'. The ECA has issued Barton Chemicals Ltd with three minor technical notices of non-compliance in twelve months — each remedied promptly and without any environmental harm — and has then revoked its licence entirely. The ECA justifies revocation by stating that 'three notices in twelve months is our threshold for persistent non-compliance and we apply it consistently to all licensees'. Barton Chemicals argues that the revocation was unlawful.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Barton Chemicals on whether and how it can challenge the revocation?

options.A: Barton Chemicals has no grounds for challenge because the ECA has acted consistently with all licensees, and equal treatment is a complete answer to any claim of unlawful discrimination in the exercise of administrative powers.
options.B: Barton Chemicals has grounds on multiple bases: construing three promptly-remedied technical notices as 'persistent failure' may be an error of law; applying the threshold policy rigidly without exception fetters discretion; and the resulting conditions are disproportionate to any legitimate aim.
options.C: Barton Chemicals has no challenge because the ECA is entitled to set and apply consistent thresholds for 'persistent non-compliance' under its statutory power.
options.D: Barton Chemicals has a viable challenge based on proportionality only; the error of law and fettering of discretion arguments are too weak because the statutory threshold of 'persistent non-compliance' is broad enough to accommodate the ECA's interpretation.
options.E: Barton Chemicals has grounds for challenge based on fettering of discretion only; errors of law by specialist environmental agencies are non-reviewable because courts defer to expert bodies.

questions.explanation: B is correct. This scenario raises three interconnected grounds. On error of law: 'persistent failure to comply' is a statutory phrase that the court can review. An interpretation that treats three minor, promptly remedied technical breaches as 'persistent failure' may be an error of law — 'persistent' implies ongoing or repeated disregard, not technical incidents corrected without harm. Following ex parte Page, all errors of law are in principle reviewable. On fettering of discretion: even if the threshold policy is a permissible interpretation, applying it rigidly 'without exception' to all cases regardless of individual circumstances is a fettering of discretion. The ECA must retain the ability to consider whether the individual circumstances of a case — particularly the minor nature of the breaches, prompt remediation and absence of harm — justify departing from the threshold. On proportionality: where Convention rights are not directly engaged, proportionality as a domestic ground is developing; but even applying Wednesbury, revoking a licence entirely for trivial, remedied, harmless technical breaches is strongly arguably disproportionate to the point of irrationality. D: Incorrect — consistent application of a threshold does not insulate it from review; both the content of the policy and its rigid application can be challenged. A: Incorrect — errors of law by specialist agencies are reviewable; specialist deference operates in relation to matters of technical expertise, not to the legal interpretation of statutory phrases. E: Incorrect — equal but unlawful treatment of all licensees does not cure an unlawful policy; consistency in applying an unlawful approach provides no defence. C: Incorrect — the error of law and fettering arguments are independent and strong; the breadth of the phrase is precisely what is disputed, and 'broadly drafted' does not mean 'immune from review'.

questions.tag: Article 8 and section 6 — positive obligations and hybrid bodies
questions.scenario: The Clarendon Care Foundation (CCF) is a registered charity operating a residential care home for elderly residents with dementia. The CCF receives 90% of its funding from public sources under a block contract with Halford City Council, which is under a statutory duty to arrange residential dementia care. Residents of the CCF have no meaningful freedom to leave and are entirely dependent on the CCF for all aspects of their daily lives. Margaret, an 84-year-old resident with advanced dementia, has been placed in an isolation room for extended periods with no explanation given to her family, who allege that this treatment breaches her rights under Art 8 ECHR.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Margaret's family on whether and how they can challenge the CCF's treatment?

options.A: The family has no basis for challenge under Art 8 because the CCF's isolation policy is a matter of clinical judgment that falls within the margin of discretion afforded to care providers, and courts cannot review clinical decisions.
options.B: The family has strong grounds — the CCF is likely a hybrid public authority under s 6(3)(b) HRA for its residential care function, given the statutory framework, public funding, and close regulation; it must therefore act compatibly with Convention rights.
options.C: The family has a strong basis for challenge, but only through the local authority's obligations under s 6 HRA; there is no direct HRA obligation on the CCF itself regardless of its public funding level.
options.D: The family has grounds for challenge under Art 8 only if the local authority — as the statutory duty holder — is named as the defendant; the CCF itself cannot be a public authority because it contracted independently with the local authority.
options.E: The family has no basis for challenge because the CCF is a registered charity, not a public body, and charities cannot be public authorities for the purposes of s 6 HRA.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The central question is whether the CCF is a public authority for HRA purposes. S 6(3)(b) extends the definition to bodies exercising 'functions of a public nature'. The CCF is not a core public authority but may be a hybrid one. The relevant factors — statutory underpinning via the local authority's duty, near-total public funding, assumption of public functions in relation to vulnerable individuals who lack autonomy — are strongly analogous to the statutory underpinning present in R (on the application of A) v Partnership in Care, where a private psychiatric hospital was found to be exercising a public function. On Art 8: the right to respect for private life encompasses personal dignity, and extended unexplained isolation of a dementia patient is directly capable of breaching Art 8. The state (via the CCF as functional public authority) must show the isolation was prescribed by law, pursued a legitimate aim, and was necessary and proportionate. D: Incorrect — charitable status does not preclude a body from being a functional public authority under s 6(3)(b); the analysis focuses on the nature of the function, not the corporate form. E: Incorrect — while the local authority retains overall responsibility, the CCF as the direct provider of care and the body actually implementing the impugned treatment is the appropriate defendant in relation to its own acts; both may have obligations. C: Incorrect — clinical decisions by public authorities are subject to Art 8 review; the margin of discretion is a factor but does not exclude judicial scrutiny of plainly unjustified treatment. A: Incorrect — if the CCF is a functional public authority under s 6(3)(b), it bears direct HRA obligations in relation to the function it is exercising; the claim is not limited to proceeding via the local authority.

questions.scenario: Carmen is a nurse employed by the Eastmere National Health Service Trust. She is also a patient at the same Trust. In two separate disputes: (1) she claims that the Trust unlawfully withdrew her contractual right to subsidised staff parking under its employment contract; and (2) she claims that the Trust failed to provide her with a statutory treatment protocol to which she was entitled as a patient under the Health Entitlements Act 2020. Carmen wishes to bring both claims in the civil courts rather than by judicial review.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Carmen on how to bring her claims?

options.A: Carmen must bring the treatment protocol claim in the civil courts because statutory entitlements create private law rights, while the parking claim must be brought by way of JR because employment decisions by public bodies are public law matters.
options.B: Carmen must bring both claims by way of judicial review because NHS Trusts are hybrid public authorities and all disputes with them, whether employment or treatment-related, are public law matters.
options.C: Carmen's two claims require separate treatment: the employment and parking claim is a private law matter for ordinary civil proceedings; the treatment protocol claim engages statutory public law entitlements and must be brought by judicial review under CPR Part 54.
options.D: Carmen can bring both claims in the civil courts because the procedural exclusivity rule in O'Reilly v Mackman was completely abolished by the Civil Procedure Rules and JR is now entirely optional.
options.E: Carmen must bring both claims by way of judicial review because both involve an NHS Trust, which is a public body, and O'Reilly v Mackman requires all public law matters to be pursued through JR.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The procedural exclusivity analysis must be applied separately to each claim. The employment/parking claim: the source of Carmen's right is her employment contract — a private law relationship. Even where the employer is a public body, a contractual right of an employee gives rise to private law proceedings; the O'Reilly exclusivity rule does not require contractual employment claims to be brought by JR (Roy v Kensington). The treatment protocol claim: the right to a statutory treatment protocol under the Health Entitlements Act 2020 is a public law right. Strictly, O'Reilly would require this to be pursued by JR to protect the safeguards of the JR procedure. However, under the modern approach in Clark v University of Lincolnshire, the court's focus is not on strict procedural adherence but on whether the proceedings are conducted justly. If no material injustice results, the claim may be allowed to proceed in ordinary civil proceedings, but the court retains a discretion to transfer. E: Incorrect — O'Reilly does not apply to private law contractual claims; it targets claims that are 'public law' in substance; the parking/employment claim is private in nature. B: Incorrect — being a public body does not convert all disputes with that body into public law matters; the nature of the right engaged determines the procedure. D: Incorrect — procedural exclusivity has not been abolished; it has been modified by a more flexible approach under the CPR, but JR remains the primary route for public law claims. A: Incorrect — this reverses the correct analysis; statutory entitlements are public law rights (potentially requiring JR), while employment contracts give rise to private law rights.

questions.scenario: Following a series of serious public security incidents, the government enacts the Public Emergency (Detention and Investigation) Act, which permits the Minister to authorise the detention without charge of individuals for up to 28 days where the Minister 'considers it necessary for the investigation of acts of terrorism.' The Act also permits the use of 'enhanced interrogation techniques' during such detention, defined in the Act as including prolonged sleep deprivation and exposure to extremes of temperature. The government declares a public emergency under Art 15 ECHR and argues this derogation permits these measures. Hamid, detained without charge for 24 days and subjected to the defined interrogation techniques, brings proceedings under the HRA.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Hamid on the strength of his challenge?

options.A: Hamid has a strong challenge to both the detention and the interrogation techniques on the basis that Art 15 derogations are never lawful under domestic HRA law, regardless of the severity of the public emergency.
options.B: Hamid has no viable challenge because the government has validly declared a public emergency under Art 15 ECHR, which permits derogation from all Convention rights during a genuine public emergency.
options.C: Hamid has grounds on two bases: 28-day detention without charge requires a valid Art 15 derogation and will likely violate Art 5 absent prompt judicial oversight; the interrogation techniques may constitute inhuman or degrading treatment under Art 3, which is absolute and non-derogable.
options.D: Hamid has grounds to challenge the detention but not the interrogation techniques, because Art 3 permits derogation in extreme national security circumstances where the emergency is severe enough.
options.E: Hamid has no challenge to the interrogation techniques because the government has defined the techniques as 'enhanced interrogation' rather than 'torture', and the domestic classification of the methods determines whether Art 3 is engaged.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The answer requires distinguishing between limited rights (from which Art 15 derogation is in principle possible) and absolute rights (from which no derogation is ever permitted). Art 5 (liberty) is a limited right; derogation is permissible in a genuine public emergency, but must be strictly necessary and proportionate — 28 days without charge requires careful scrutiny and may exceed what is 'strictly required by the exigencies of the situation'. Art 3 is an absolute right. The ECtHR has confirmed unequivocally that Art 3 admits of no exceptions and no derogation under Art 15, regardless of the gravity of the public emergency. Prolonged sleep deprivation and exposure to extreme temperatures are well-established forms of inhuman or degrading treatment and fall within Art 3. The government's derogation cannot authorise Art 3 treatment, and domestic legislation requiring or authorising such treatment cannot be validated by a purported Art 15 derogation. E: Incorrect — Art 15 does not permit derogation from Art 3; the list of non-derogable rights in Art 15(2) includes Art 3. A: Incorrect — the ECtHR does not accept that domestic classification of treatment as something other than 'torture' determines whether Art 3 is engaged; it makes its own autonomous assessment of whether treatment is tortuous, inhuman or degrading. C: Incorrect — Art 15 derogations are in principle lawful under the HRA (ss 14-16 HRA); the point is that they cannot authorise Art 3 treatment. B: Incorrect — Art 3 is absolute without exception; national security, however extreme, cannot justify conduct falling within its scope.

questions.scenario: The Northgate Bar Standards Panel (NBSP) is a statutory body responsible for determining complaints against solicitors. Three members of a five-person NBSP panel are considering a complaint against Sofia, a solicitor. Member A conducted the preliminary investigation into the complaint and produced a written report recommending that Sofia be struck off as 'guilty beyond doubt.' Member B is the former managing partner of the law firm that made the complaint against Sofia. Member C has no connection to the parties. Sofia's lawyer objects to the participation of both Member A and Member B before the hearing begins.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Sofia on the strength of her objections?

options.A: Sofia has objections to both members: Member A produced a preliminary report concluding Sofia is 'guilty beyond doubt', creating a real possibility of bias or predetermination; Member B's financial interest in a competing operator creates an apparent conflict of interest.
options.B: Sofia has no objection to either member because the Panel has a three-member majority without Member A and Member B, so any decision is unaffected by their participation.
options.C: Sofia has a strong objection to Member B's participation but not to Member A's; a preliminary investigation and report is an inherent part of the Panel's statutory process and cannot itself found a bias challenge, since all Panel members must be familiar with the case before hearing it.
options.D: Sofia has a strong objection to Member A's participation but not to Member B's; a former professional connection to the complaining firm is too remote to satisfy the Porter v Magill test, and the prior connection would have to be current and direct to found a bias challenge.
options.E: Sofia's objections are both likely to fail because the NBSP has internal expertise and the courts should defer to its own assessments of its members' impartiality.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The Porter v Magill test asks whether the fair-minded and informed observer, having considered the facts, would conclude there was a real possibility that the tribunal was biased. Member A: writing a report concluding Sofia is 'guilty beyond doubt' is not a provisional or preliminary view — it is a settled, expressed conclusion on the very question the Panel must now decide. This is precisely the situation addressed in R v Kent Police Authority ex parte Godden, where a doctor who had previously formed a view on the relevant question was held to be disqualified. A: Incorrect — internal deference does not override the fair-minded observer test; the courts apply Porter v Magill to all public decision-making bodies. B on Member E is correct. Member B: being the former managing partner of the complaining firm is a prior institutional allegiance that is directly relevant to the complaint. Even if the connection is no longer current, a fair-minded observer would question whether it creates a real possibility of partiality; this is stronger than mere membership of an organisation (contrast Helow, where mere membership without personal expressed views was insufficient). D: Incorrect — a former managing partner role with the complaining firm is a more direct and significant connection than mere membership of an association (Helow); the objection to Member B is strong, not weak. C: Incorrect — Member A did not merely read the file; they made an expressed written finding of guilt. This goes far beyond the investigative familiarisation that is inherent in the process. E: Incorrect — the taint of an interested member's participation is not cured by the existence of a majority of uninterested members; the participation of a biased member taints the entire proceedings regardless of voting arithmetic.

questions.scenario: In 2018, the Veldmore High Court ruled — for the first time — that the offence of 'criminal facilitation' under the Organised Crime Prevention Act 2010 extended to persons who provided financial services to organisations later shown to have been engaged in criminal activity, even where those services were provided without knowledge of the criminal purpose. Applying this ruling, the Crown Prosecution Service charged Oscar with criminal facilitation for providing legitimate accountancy services in 2015 to a company that was subsequently revealed to have been running an investment fraud. Oscar argues that at the time he provided the services, no court had ever held that the offence extended to unknowing facilitators, and that his conviction would constitute retrospective criminalisation in breach of Art 7 ECHR.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to Oscar on the strength of his Art 7 challenge?

options.A: Oscar has a potential Art 7 challenge — Art 7 prohibits retrospective criminalisation, but permits foreseeable judicial development of the law; the question is whether the 2018 ruling was a foreseeable clarification or an unforeseeable extension of liability.
options.B: Oscar has a strong Art 7 challenge based on the principle that criminal law must be prescribed by statute, not judge-made, and all common law developments in criminal law therefore violate Art 7.
options.C: Oscar has no Art 7 challenge because Art 7 only applies to statutory provisions that are enacted retrospectively; judicial interpretation of existing legislation is not subject to Art 7.
options.D: Oscar has a strong Art 7 challenge because Art 7 requires that criminal offences be defined in advance by statute, and a judicial extension of the scope of an existing offence is always a retrospective criminalisation in violation of Art 7.
options.E: Oscar has no Art 7 challenge because the offence existed in statute at the time of his conduct; it is irrelevant that the courts had not previously interpreted the offence in the way applied to him.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Art 7 does not prohibit the progressive development and clarification of criminal law through judicial interpretation; such development has existed for centuries, particularly in common law systems. The ECtHR in SW v UK held that the clarification of the law on marital rape by the courts did not violate Art 7 because the development followed a 'perceptible line of case-law development' that was 'reasonably foreseeable' and 'consistent with the very essence of the offence.' The key questions for Oscar's case are: (1) Was the 2018 ruling a foreseeable development of the existing law on facilitation, or an unpredictable departure? (2) Could Oscar, by taking appropriate legal advice, have foreseen that his conduct might fall within the statutory offence as it would evolve? If the 2018 ruling was a sudden, unforeseeable extension — particularly one that criminalises persons who lacked any knowledge of the criminal purpose — the foreseeability requirement may not have been satisfied. B: Incorrect — Art 7 does not absolutely prohibit all judicial extensions of criminal law; it permits foreseeable clarification and incremental development; the test is accessibility and foreseeability, not whether the development was judicial rather than statutory. C: Incorrect — Art 7 expressly encompasses criminal liability under 'national or international law', which includes both statute and common law/judicial interpretation. A: Incorrect — the existence of a statute is not sufficient; Art 7 requires that its application to the specific conduct in question was foreseeable at the time of the conduct. D: Incorrect — common law development of criminal law is not automatically a violation of Art 7; the ECtHR has repeatedly confirmed that foreseeable judicial interpretation is compatible with Art 7.

questions.scenario: Craig is a local resident who successfully challenges a decision by the Northmere District Council to grant planning permission for a large warehouse on green-belt land near his home. The Administrative Court finds that the Council failed to consider a material planning consideration when making its decision, rendering the grant of permission unlawful. However, the court also notes that even had the Council properly considered the factor, it is highly likely that the outcome would not have been substantially different, as the factor was of relatively minor significance. Craig seeks a quashing order to invalidate the planning permission.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the court's likely approach to Craig's application for a quashing order?

options.A: The court must grant the quashing order because a finding of unlawfulness automatically entitles the claimant to the remedy sought.
options.B: The court must refuse the quashing order because only the decision-maker, not the court, has the power to revisit the planning permission.
options.C: The court may refuse to grant the quashing order under its discretionary powers, but cannot be compelled to do so by statute even where the outcome would not have been substantially different.
options.D: The court must refuse to grant relief if it appears highly likely that the outcome for the applicant would not have been substantially different had the unlawful conduct not occurred, unless there are reasons of exceptional public interest.
options.E: The court should grant the quashing order because the discretionary nature of remedies only applies to mandatory orders and declarations, not to quashing orders.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under s 31(2A) Senior Courts Act 1981 (as amended by the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015), the court must refuse to grant relief on a judicial review application if it appears highly likely that the outcome for the applicant would not have been substantially different had the conduct complained of not occurred — this is the 'not substantially different' test. A: Incorrect — remedies in judicial review are discretionary in nature (Walton v Scottish Ministers) and there is no automatic entitlement to a remedy upon a finding of unlawfulness. B: Incorrect — the court does have the power to grant quashing orders, which invalidate the impugned decision and require the public body to take the decision again; the court is not substituting its own decision. C: Incorrect — s 31(2A) does compel the court to refuse relief where the 'not substantially different' test is met, unless there are reasons of exceptional public interest. E: Incorrect — the discretionary nature of remedies applies to all judicial review remedies including quashing orders, prohibitory orders and mandatory orders.

questions.scenario: Maria is a market trader who holds a licence from the Dunfield Borough Council to operate a food stall in the town centre. Following a complaint from a member of the public, the Council's Licensing Committee decides to revoke her licence. Maria receives a letter informing her that her licence has been revoked with immediate effect. The letter does not disclose the nature of the complaint or the evidence upon which the Committee relied. Maria was not given an opportunity to respond before the decision was made.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the Council has breached its duty to act fairly in revoking Maria's licence?

options.A: The Council has breached its duty to act fairly because Maria was not given notice of the case against her or an opportunity to make representations before the decision was taken, and as a licence-holder she was entitled to a higher level of procedural protection than a mere applicant.
options.B: The Council has not breached its duty to act fairly because a market trader's licence is a privilege, not a right, and so the Council has no obligation to disclose the reasons for its revocation.
options.C: The Council has breached its duty to act fairly, but only because it failed to provide written reasons after the decision; the lack of prior notice is not relevant since the Council was not under an obligation to consult before revoking a licence.
options.D: The Council has not breached its duty to act fairly because it acted on the basis of a complaint from a member of the public and was entitled to protect the complainant's identity by withholding the evidence.
options.E: The Council has breached its duty to act fairly, but only if Maria can demonstrate that, had she been given notice and the opportunity to respond, the outcome would have been different.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The most basic element of procedural fairness is that a person must be given notice of the case against them and the opportunity to make representations in response. Failure to inform a person of the evidence against them means they cannot prepare their case properly (R v Governing Body of Dunraven School, ex parte B). Under the McInnes v Onslow-Fane spectrum, a person facing forfeiture of an existing licence is entitled to a higher level of fairness than a mere applicant. B: Incorrect — even though a licence may be characterised as a privilege, procedural fairness still applies to its revocation, particularly where the holder faces forfeiture of something they already possess. C: Incorrect — while the absence of reasons may also be problematic, the primary breach here is the failure to give prior notice and an opportunity to respond before the decision was made; both are fundamental aspects of fairness. D: Incorrect — while there may be circumstances where source protection is justified, the complete withholding of the nature of the complaint and all evidence is a clear breach of the duty to act fairly (R v Chief Constable of North Wales Police, ex parte Thorpe distinguished — there the failure to disclose did not make a material difference, unlike here). E: Incorrect — it is not necessary for the claimant to prove the outcome would have been different; the breach lies in the denial of the procedural right itself.

questions.tag: Content of fairness — right to an oral hearing
questions.scenario: Edward is a prisoner who has been recalled to prison following the revocation of his release licence. He writes to the Sentence Review Panel requesting an oral hearing so that he can challenge the revocation in person, arguing that the evidence against him is disputed and that the written evidence alone does not adequately convey the circumstances. The Panel refuses his request and decides the matter entirely on the papers, without giving reasons for the refusal of an oral hearing.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the Panel's refusal to hold an oral hearing breaches the requirements of procedural fairness?

options.A: The Panel's refusal does not breach procedural fairness because there is no automatic common law right to an oral hearing, and the Panel has discretion to decide all cases on the papers if it considers this appropriate.
options.B: The Panel's refusal does not breach procedural fairness because oral hearings are only required in criminal trials, not in administrative decision-making processes.
options.C: The Panel's refusal is likely to breach procedural fairness because, where the Panel would be assisted by an oral hearing in assessing disputed facts and risk, common law fairness requires that an oral hearing be offered, and the Panel should guard against refusing hearings simply to save time, trouble and expense.
options.D: The Panel's refusal breaches procedural fairness only if Edward can show that the outcome would have been different had an oral hearing been held.
options.E: The Panel's refusal breaches procedural fairness because every prisoner who is recalled to prison following revocation of their licence has an absolute right to an oral hearing.

questions.explanation: C is correct. In R (Smith and West) v Parole Board, the court held that while the Board was not required to hold an oral hearing in every case, where it would be assisted in its task of assessing risk by having one, it should be done. This was further emphasised in Osborn v Parole Board, where the Supreme Court stressed the central importance of common law fairness and held that the Board should guard against refusing oral hearings as a means of saving time, trouble and expense. A: Incorrect — while there is no automatic right to an oral hearing, where facts are disputed and an oral hearing would assist the decision-maker, fairness may require one. B: Incorrect — the right to an oral hearing is not limited to criminal trials; it can arise in administrative proceedings depending on the circumstances (ex parte Tarrant factors). D: Incorrect — procedural fairness does not require the claimant to demonstrate the outcome would have been different; the breach is the denial of a fair procedure. E: Incorrect — there is no absolute right to an oral hearing in every recall case; the requirement depends on the circumstances of the individual case.

questions.scenario: The Regional Transport Authority (RTA) is required under the Greenshire Transport Regulation Act 2019 to consult with local residents before approving the construction of a new bus depot. Section 14 of the Act states that the RTA 'shall give notice to all affected households and allow a period of not less than 28 days for written representations.' The RTA publishes a notice on its website and in the local newspaper but does not send individual notices to affected households. It allows 28 days for representations and receives several responses. The RTA then approves the construction. Thomas, a local resident who did not see the website notice or newspaper advertisement, seeks judicial review of the decision.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the court's likely approach to the RTA's failure to comply with the statutory consultation requirements?

options.A: The court will automatically invalidate the RTA's decision because any failure to comply with a statutory procedural requirement expressed in mandatory terms renders the decision void.
options.B: The court will uphold the RTA's decision because the statutory requirement to give notice was directory rather than mandatory, meaning non-compliance does not affect the validity of the decision.
options.C: The court will consider whether, in the circumstances, the RTA's alternative method of giving notice substantially achieved the purpose of the statutory requirement, and will only invalidate the decision if the non-compliance undermined the objective that the requirement was designed to serve.
options.D: The court will invalidate the decision because the use of the word 'shall' in the statute creates an absolute obligation that admits of no flexibility in its application.
options.E: The court will uphold the decision because the RTA provided an alternative form of notice and Thomas had the opportunity to monitor the RTA's website for planning updates.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under the modern approach in R v Soneji, the courts use a flexible approach focusing on the consequences of non-compliance, asking whether Parliament would have intended that the outcome of non-compliance should be invalidity. The court will consider whether the purpose of the requirement was substantially achieved despite the technical non-compliance (see also JN (Cameroon) v SSHD, where a deportation notice that did not specify the destination country did not invalidate the decision because the omission did not impair the person's ability to appeal). A: Incorrect — the modern approach has moved away from the rigid mandatory/directory distinction; non-compliance does not automatically invalidate a decision even where the statutory language is mandatory. B: Incorrect — this reflects the old approach; the modern approach does not simply classify requirements as directory and thereby dismiss non-compliance, but focuses on the consequences of that non-compliance. D: Incorrect — the use of 'shall' does not create an absolute rule under the modern flexible approach; what matters is whether the purpose of the requirement was achieved. E: Incorrect — the obligation is on the RTA to comply with the statutory requirement, not on the resident to monitor the authority's website.

questions.scenario:
The Eastwick Metropolitan Council is considering a planning application by Harland Developments Ltd to build a large retail park on land designated for mixed use under the Eastwick Local Development Framework. Section 42 of the Eastwick Planning Governance Act 2020 requires the Council's Planning Committee to determine such applications 'having regard to all material considerations and representations received during the statutory consultation period.'

Before the consultation period ends, Councillor Davies — the Chair of the Planning Committee — gives an interview to the Eastwick Herald in which she says: 'This retail park will bring hundreds of jobs to Eastwick and I'm fully committed to seeing it approved. I've spoken with the developer and I'm confident the plans are sound.' During the Committee meeting, Councillor Davies chairs the proceedings and votes in favour of the application, which is approved.

Green Fields Action Group, a local residents' association that submitted objections during the consultation period, seeks judicial review of the decision. The Group argues that Councillor Davies's public statements demonstrate that she had a closed mind when the decision was made, rendering the process biased.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to the Green Fields Action Group on whether and how they can challenge the above decision?

options.A: The challenge is unlikely to succeed. A predisposition on the part of a local councillor in relation to a planning application is not unlawful; councillors are entitled to have opinions and to take a general political position. Under R (Lewis) v Persimmon Homes (Teeside) Ltd, only if Councillor Davies can be shown to have had a completely 'closed mind' in relation to this specific application — meaning she would not have been open to changing her view regardless of what emerged during the consultation — could her participation invalidate the decision. The statements, while strong, may fall short of demonstrating a closed mind as opposed to a predisposition, and s 25(2) of the Localism Act 2011 further protects councillors by providing that previous indications of a view do not of themselves show a closed mind.
options.B: The challenge will succeed on the ground of direct bias. Councillor Davies has a direct personal interest in the outcome of the application because she has spoken with the developer, and under the principle in Dimes v Grand Junction Canal, any direct pecuniary interest — no matter how small — automatically disqualifies a decision-maker. Her participation on the Committee invalidates the entire decision.
options.C: The challenge will succeed because the Porter v Magill test for indirect bias is met. A fair-minded and informed observer would inevitably conclude that Councillor Davies had already decided the matter before the consultation period ended, and the fact she chaired the meeting means the entire process is tainted. The Localism Act 2011 does not apply because it only protects councillors who have expressed views in the past, not those who have made commitments to developers.
options.D: The challenge is unlikely to succeed because Councillor Davies is not exercising a judicial function. The rule against bias only applies to judges and members of tribunals, not to members of a local authority planning committee. Since the decision is administrative rather than judicial, allegations of bias cannot form the basis of a judicial review claim.
options.E: The challenge will succeed because the Council has breached the statutory requirement under s 42 to have regard to all material considerations. Councillor Davies's public commitment to the application demonstrates that the Committee did not genuinely consider the objections submitted during consultation, and the decision must be quashed on the ground of illegality rather than bias.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under R (Lewis) v Persimmon Homes (Teeside) Ltd, the Court of Appeal held that a predisposition of local councillors in relation to a planning application was not unlawful — they were entitled to have an opinion and justified in taking a general political position. Only if they could be shown to have had a 'closed mind' in relation to the specific application could the decision be quashed for bias. Section 25(2) of the Localism Act 2011 reinforces this by providing that a decision-maker will not be taken to have had a closed mind just because they had previously indicated what view they took or might take. While Councillor Davies's statements are strong, they may represent a predisposition rather than a completely closed mind. B: Incorrect — there is no evidence that Councillor Davies has a direct pecuniary interest; having spoken with a developer does not establish a financial interest. The principle in Dimes requires a direct financial interest, which is not present here. C: Incorrect — while Porter v Magill provides the test for indirect bias, the mere expression of a strong opinion does not inevitably mean a fair-minded observer would conclude there was a real possibility of bias, particularly given the protections in the Localism Act 2011 and the distinction between predisposition and a closed mind. The Localism Act does apply to local authority councillors' previous indications of a view. D: Incorrect — the rule against bias applies to all public decision-making bodies, not just judges and tribunals; Porter v Magill itself states that the test applies to all public decision-making. E: Incorrect — while the statutory requirement to consider material considerations is relevant, the question of whether the Committee genuinely considered objections is separate from the bias point; the mere fact that the Chair expressed a strong opinion does not automatically mean the Committee failed to consider objections.

questions.scenario: The Westgate Housing Authority publishes a leaflet for tenants in its social housing estates stating: 'We are committed to keeping rents affordable and will do our best to ensure that no tenant faces an increase of more than 3% per year.' Twelve months later, the Authority increases rents by 8% across all its estates, citing unexpected maintenance costs. Priya, a tenant who relied on the leaflet when choosing not to move to cheaper private accommodation, challenges the increase on the ground that the Authority has frustrated her legitimate expectation.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the likely outcome of Priya's claim that a legitimate expectation arose from the leaflet?

options.A: A legitimate expectation has arisen because any published statement by a public authority directed at a class of affected persons is capable of generating a legitimate expectation, regardless of its precise wording.
options.B: A legitimate expectation has not arisen because the language of the leaflet is not 'clear, unambiguous and devoid of relevant qualification'; the phrases 'committed to' and 'will do our best' indicate an aspiration rather than a binding promise.
options.C: A legitimate expectation has arisen because Priya relied on the leaflet to her detriment by not moving to cheaper accommodation, and detrimental reliance alone is sufficient to establish a legitimate expectation.
options.D: A legitimate expectation has not arisen because housing rent levels are a matter of economic policy and public authorities can never generate legitimate expectations in relation to financial matters.
options.E: A legitimate expectation has arisen because the leaflet was published by the Authority and directed to its tenants, satisfying the requirement that an expectation can only be generated by or on behalf of a public authority.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under R v IRC, ex parte MFK Underwriting Agents Ltd, a promise must be 'clear, unambiguous and devoid of relevant qualification' to generate a legitimate expectation. The phrases 'committed to' and 'will do our best' are aspirational and qualified, not constituting a clear, binding promise. Under Paponette, the question is how, on a fair reading, the promise would have been reasonably understood by those to whom it was made — here, the language suggests best endeavours rather than a guarantee. A: Incorrect — not every published statement by a public authority generates a legitimate expectation; the statement must be sufficiently clear and unambiguous. C: Incorrect — while detrimental reliance is a relevant factor, it is not sufficient on its own to establish a legitimate expectation; the representation itself must first be sufficiently clear (R v SoS for Education and Employment, ex parte Begbie). D: Incorrect — there is no blanket rule that public authorities cannot generate legitimate expectations in relation to financial matters; it depends on the clarity and context of the representation. E: Incorrect — while it is true that a legitimate expectation can only be generated by or on behalf of a public authority, this is a necessary but not sufficient condition; the representation must also be clear and unambiguous.

questions.scenario: The Ashford Regional Health Board has, for the past fifteen years, consulted with patient representative groups before making changes to the delivery of mental health services in the region. This practice has been followed consistently by successive Board chairs and is widely known among service users. Without any prior warning, the Board announces a restructuring of community mental health teams with significant changes to service delivery, without consulting the patient representative groups. The Ashford Mental Health Alliance, a patient representative group, challenges the decision.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the Alliance can establish that a legitimate expectation has arisen?

options.A: A legitimate expectation has not arisen because the Board's past practice of consultation was purely voluntary and never required by statute, so the Board was always free to depart from it without notice.
options.B: A legitimate expectation has not arisen because only express promises, not past practices, are capable of generating legitimate expectations.
options.C: A legitimate expectation has arisen because the consistent and long-standing practice of consultation has generated a procedural legitimate expectation that the Board will consult before making changes, even though no express promise was made.
options.D: A legitimate expectation has arisen, but only a substantive legitimate expectation that the Board will not change mental health services at all, entitling the Alliance to the continuation of existing services.
options.E: A legitimate expectation has not arisen because the Alliance is a pressure group and only individuals directly affected by a decision can rely on the doctrine of legitimate expectation.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under GCHQ, Lord Fraser stated that a legitimate expectation may arise 'from the existence of a regular practice which the claimant can reasonably expect to continue.' The Board's fifteen-year consistent practice of consulting patient representative groups before making changes to mental health services is analogous to the settled practice in GCHQ. This generates a procedural legitimate expectation that the groups will be consulted. A: Incorrect — a legitimate expectation can arise from an established practice regardless of whether it was required by statute; the key is that the practice was regular and settled (GCHQ; R v IRC, ex parte Unilever plc). B: Incorrect — Lord Fraser in GCHQ expressly recognised that legitimate expectations can arise from regular practice as well as express promises. D: Incorrect — the expectation arising from a practice of consultation is procedural (i.e. the right to be consulted), not substantive (i.e. the right to the continuation of existing services). E: Incorrect — there is no rule limiting the doctrine of legitimate expectation to individuals; groups that were the recipients of the practice or promise can rely on it.

questions.scenario: The Millbrook Environmental Agency, which regulates waste disposal under the Millbrook Environmental Protection Act 2018, writes to GreenCycle Ltd assuring the company that it will be exempt from new emission standards introduced under s 45 of the Act. Section 45 contains no provision for exemptions and imposes the standards on all waste disposal operators without exception. When the Agency subsequently enforces the new standards against GreenCycle, the company argues that it had a legitimate expectation of exemption based on the Agency's written assurance.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether GreenCycle can rely on the Agency's assurance to establish a legitimate expectation?

options.A: GreenCycle can rely on the assurance because any written promise by a public authority is binding, regardless of whether the authority had the legal power to make it.
options.B: GreenCycle cannot rely on the assurance because the Agency had no legal power to grant an exemption from the statutory requirements under s 45; a representation must be legal in the sense that it would be within the authority's powers to fulfil the promise.
options.C: GreenCycle can rely on the assurance because the Agency made the promise in its capacity as the statutory regulator, and regulators have implied powers to grant exemptions.
options.D: GreenCycle cannot rely on the assurance, but only because it did not suffer detrimental reliance; had it changed its operations in reliance on the promise, the expectation would be enforceable regardless of the Agency's legal powers.
options.E: GreenCycle can rely on the assurance because the doctrine of good administration requires public authorities to honour their promises, even where those promises exceed their statutory powers.

questions.explanation: B is correct. A representation must be legal in the sense that it would be within the public authority's powers to fulfil the promise. Under R v SoS for Education and Employment, ex parte Begbie, an expectation was not held to be legitimate in part because fulfilling the promise would have been contrary to statutory requirements. Similarly, in Rowland v Environment Agency, the Court of Appeal held that since there was no statutory power to extinguish public access rights, the claimant could not found a legitimate expectation on the Agency's past conduct. Here, s 45 admits no exemptions, so the Agency had no power to grant one. A: Incorrect — a written promise is not automatically binding; it must be within the authority's powers to fulfil it. C: Incorrect — regulators do not have implied powers to grant exemptions from mandatory statutory requirements; their powers are defined by the statute. D: Incorrect — even if detrimental reliance existed, a legitimate expectation cannot arise from an ultra vires representation. E: Incorrect — while good administration is a relevant principle, it does not override the requirement that the representation must be lawful; a promise that exceeds statutory powers cannot be enforced.

questions.scenario: The Department for Community Services introduces an internal policy providing that applicants for disability support grants who submit their applications within 30 days of diagnosis will receive priority processing. The policy is not published on the Department's website and is not communicated to applicants. Fatima applies for a grant 45 days after her diagnosis and is placed on a standard waiting list. She later discovers the policy and argues that she had a legitimate expectation of priority processing, as she would have applied earlier had she known about it.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Fatima can establish a legitimate expectation based on the internal policy?

options.A: Fatima can establish a legitimate expectation because the Department had a duty to publicise all internal policies that affect applicants' rights, and its failure to do so cannot be held against her.
options.B: Fatima cannot establish a legitimate expectation in the traditional sense because, as a general rule, a person must have knowledge of the representation or practice at the relevant time in order to rely on it, and she had no such knowledge when she made her application.
options.C: Fatima can establish a legitimate expectation because the doctrine of legitimate expectation is wholly objective and never requires knowledge on the part of the individual claimant.
options.D: Fatima cannot establish a legitimate expectation because internal policies can never generate legitimate expectations; only representations made directly to individuals or published policies are capable of doing so.
options.E: Fatima can establish a legitimate expectation because she suffered detriment by not applying within 30 days, and detrimental reliance is the only requirement for establishing a legitimate expectation.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The general rule is that a person must have knowledge of the representation or past practice at the relevant time in order to rely on it (R v Ministry of Defence, ex parte Walker; SoS for the Home Department v Rahman). However, Fatima may have an alternative argument under the separate doctrine of consistency in application of policy (Lumba; Mandalia), which requires public authorities to apply their own policies consistently — though this is distinct from the doctrine of legitimate expectation. A: Incorrect — while there may be arguments about good administration, the doctrine of legitimate expectation generally requires the claimant to have knowledge of the representation. B is correct as the general rule, though limited exceptions exist. C: Incorrect — while ex parte Ahmed and Patel suggested that legitimate expectation is a 'wholly objective concept' in the immigration context, the general rule still requires knowledge; the immigration cases represent a specific exception, not the general position. D: Incorrect — internal policies can generate legitimate expectations if they are communicated to or known by the affected individuals (AG of Hong Kong v Ng Yuen Shiu). E: Incorrect — detrimental reliance is not the sole requirement; knowledge of the representation is generally required as a precondition.

questions.scenario: The Daleston Borough Council has for eight years operated a scheme providing free after-school tutoring for children in low-income households across the borough. The scheme was established following a public announcement by the then-Council Leader who stated: 'Every child in a low-income household in Daleston will have access to free after-school tutoring for as long as this Council is responsible for education.' The Council now proposes to abolish the scheme entirely, citing severe budget constraints following central government funding cuts. Several parents challenge the decision.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the court's likely approach to assessing whether the Council can lawfully frustrate the parents' legitimate expectation?

options.A: The court will apply a bare Wednesbury unreasonableness test and will only intervene if the Council's decision was so unreasonable that no reasonable authority would have made it.
options.B: The court will assess whether the frustration of the expectation is a proportionate response to a legitimate aim pursued by the Council in the public interest, weighing the force of the expectation against the public interest reasons advanced for departing from it.
options.C: The court cannot review the Council's decision at all because decisions about the allocation of public resources are inherently non-justiciable.
options.D: The court will automatically enforce the expectation because the original promise was clear and unambiguous, and a public body can never depart from a clear substantive promise.
options.E: The court will only allow the Council to frustrate the expectation if there has been a change in the law requiring the abolition of the scheme; budgetary constraints can never justify the frustration of a legitimate expectation.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under R (Nadarajah and Abdi) v SoS for the Home Department, Laws LJ held that a proportionality approach should be adopted to determine whether both procedural and substantive legitimate expectations can be lawfully frustrated. The public body's promise may only be denied where to do so is a proportionate response having regard to a legitimate aim pursued in the public interest. The court will weigh the strength of the expectation (clear promise, possible detrimental reliance, made to a specific group) against the public interest reasons (severe budget constraints). A: Incorrect — while the Wednesbury test was the earlier approach (Re Findlay; ex parte Hargreaves), the modern approach following Nadarajah and Abdi and the development in Coughlan is one of proportionality, not bare rationality. C: Incorrect — while the courts give greater deference in macro-political decisions (ex parte Begbie, Laws LJ), decisions about the allocation of resources are not inherently non-justiciable. D: Incorrect — even a clear and unambiguous promise can be departed from if the frustration is proportionate to a legitimate aim in the public interest. E: Incorrect — budgetary constraints can constitute a legitimate reason for frustrating an expectation, though the court will assess whether the response is proportionate.

questions.scenario:
The Redbourne County Council operates several residential care facilities for elderly residents with complex needs. In 2019, the Council opened a new purpose-built facility, Meadowbank House, and wrote individually to fourteen residents being transferred there from an older facility stating: 'Meadowbank House will be your permanent home for as long as you need residential care. We will not close this facility or require you to move.'

Section 28 of the Redbourne Social Care Act 2021 provides: 'The Council shall provide residential care accommodation for persons within its area who are assessed as needing such accommodation, having regard to available resources and the efficient provision of services.'

In 2026, the Council decides to close Meadowbank House and transfer all residents to a larger, newly built facility fifteen miles away, citing the need to consolidate services and reduce costs following a 25% reduction in central government funding. The fourteen residents challenge the decision on the ground of legitimate expectation.
questions.stem: Which one of the following represents the best advice to the fourteen residents on whether and how they can challenge the Council's decision?
